figures.gloss: The Supreme Personality of Godhead in A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada's ISKCON. He is not an avatar of Vishnu, but the absolute, original source of all avatars, universes, and spiritual energies, reigning in the highest spiritual planet, Goloka Vrindavana.
figures.facet: ISKCON radically inverts Advaita Vedanta: the formless, infinite Brahman is merely the blinding rays of Krishna's localized, blue-hued, flute-playing transcendental body. Personality is superior to formlessness.
figures.provenance: A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada's 'Bhagavad-gita As It Is' (1968).

figures.gloss: The Supreme Soul in the Brahma Kumaris tradition. He is an incorporeal point of divine, reddish-golden light dwelling in the Soul World (Paramdham), entirely beyond the cycle of birth, death, and human degradation.
figures.facet: He is completely stripped of traditional Hindu mythological iconography. He never incarnates biologically, but merely possesses the body of the founder, Brahma Baba, to speak directly to the 'Brahmin' initiates.

figures.gloss: Maharishi Mahesh Yogi's reinterpretation of pure consciousness. It is the fundamental, unmanifest level of nature's intelligence from which all matter and energy arise, accessible directly through the effortless mental technique of Transcendental Meditation.
figures.facet: Spiritual liberation is explicitly scientized and stripped of religious devotion. Achieving cosmic consciousness is framed as a measurable neurophysiological shift governed by quantum physics and natural law.
figures.provenance: Maharishi Mahesh Yogi's 'Science of Being and Art of Living' (1963).

figures.gloss: Osho's vision of the ultimate 'New Man'—an integrated being who completely unites the sensual, earthly joyousness of 'Zorba the Greek' with the silent, detached awareness of Gautama Buddha.
figures.facet: It violently rejects the foundational asceticism of traditional Indian monasticism. Enlightenment is achieved not by renouncing wealth, sex, and material pleasure, but by moving through them with total, meditative awareness.
figures.provenance: Bhagwan Shree Rajneesh's (Osho) 'Zorba the Buddha' discourses (1970s-80s).

figures.gloss: The primordial, maternal spiritual energy lying dormant at the base of the spine. In Shri Mataji Nirmala Devi's Sahaja Yoga, it is awakened to pierce the fontanel, connecting the individual to the all-pervading power of divine love.
figures.facet: Historically a dangerous, esoteric fire requiring decades of extreme ascetic preparation, it is democratized into instant, mass 'Self-Realization' bestowed freely in public auditoriums with no prior purification.
figures.provenance: Shri Mataji Nirmala Devi's 'Meta Modern Era' (1995).

figures.gloss: The deathless, supreme Himalayan master of the Self-Realization Fellowship lineage. Retaining the physical form of a twenty-five-year-old youth for centuries, he operates in secret to guide the spiritual evolution of humanity.
figures.facet: He acts as a historical bridge, deliberately blending ancient Indian yogic science with Western esoteric expectations of ascended masters, transmitting the specific salvation mechanism of 'Kriya Yoga' to the modern world.

figures.gloss: In Ananda Marga, the rare, singular incarnation of the Supreme Entity who physically enters the material world when dharma is entirely lost, acting as both a spiritual preceptor and a fierce social liberator.
figures.facet: Unlike a passive guru, this figure demands radical, militant socio-economic revolution (PROUT) alongside deep tantric meditation, destroying oppressive capitalist and communist structures to establish a moral, spiritual society.

figures.gloss: The Brahma Kumaris' conceptualization of cosmic time as an eternally repeating, identical World Drama. Humanity begins in a pure Golden Age, degrading through Silver, Copper, and Iron ages before the apocalyptic destruction and immediate renewal.
figures.facet: The traditional Hindu cycle of millions of years is hyper-compressed into exactly 5,000 years. It is absolute determinism: every single soul repeats their exact thoughts and actions every cycle, forever.
figures.provenance: Brahma Kumaris' core curriculum and channeled 'Murlis' (1930s-present).

edges.note: Intra-tradition bond: Shiva Baba must forcefully intervene in the material world at the absolute end of the Kalpa cycle (the Confluence Age) to destroy the old tree and plant the seed of the new one.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: ISKCON explicitly subordinates the traditional formless Brahman, redefining it merely as the shining bodily effulgence radiating from Krishna's supreme personal form.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Yogananda explicitly taught that Mahavatar Babaji and Jesus Christ work together in continuous communion to send the science of Kriya Yoga to save the West.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent an underlying, unmanifest totality of pure potential and consciousness from which all material reality and localized minds continuously emanate.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Osho's ideal explicitly rejects all spirit-matter dualism and asceticism, demanding the simultaneous indulgence of earthly material desires and absolute spiritual detachment.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both envision the ultimate divine as a singular, invisible point of pure, unblemished spiritual essence existing entirely outside the corrupted material cycle.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: While both use the term kalpa to denote cosmic time, the Brahma Kumaris compress this into a hyper-fatalistic, infinitely repeating cycle of exactly 5,000 years.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent an innate, dormant piece of divine potential residing within the human form, requiring specific esoteric triggering to awaken, rise, and grant liberation.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are supreme incarnate figures destined to intervene at the climax of historical degradation, ruthlessly destroying systemic corruption and restoring cosmic moral order.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both function as an impersonal, omnipresent, un-namable fundamental law of nature that spontaneously harmonizes all existence when a practitioner aligns with it effortlessly.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both utilize the serpent archetype as the primordial, animating current of life energy, though one is internalized in the human spine and the other externalized in the landscape.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are envisioned as exclusively supreme, hyper-personal creators who demand absolute devotion and operate totally distinct from the pantheons of other gods.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Osho's concept mirrors the Daoist integration of opposites, positing that a complete human must perfectly balance materialistic celebration and silent meditation.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both function as immortal, mythic originators of esoteric sciences, dwelling outside normal historical time to disseminate ancient transformative techniques to elite initiates.

dossier.essence: A cluster of modern new religious movements that grew from Hindu, yogic, Vedantic, and tantric roots (from the later nineteenth century onward) and globalized, especially into the West — among them Transcendental Meditation, the Brahma Kumaris, Self-Realization Fellowship, Ananda Marga, Osho/Rajneesh, Sahaja Yoga, and ISKCON (the Hare Krishnas). They typically pair a charismatic living guru or founder-vessel of the divine with a portable spiritual technique — meditation, mantra, kriya, or kundalini awakening — offered across cultures. They are not one doctrine but a family sharing this reformist, exportable shape.
dossier.cosmos: Cosmologies differ sharply. ISKCON teaches a personalist Vaishnava theism in which Krishna is the supreme source and eternal souls transmigrate until liberated by devotion; the Brahma Kumaris hold a soul–matter dualism and a fatalistic 5,000-year world-cycle with God as an incorporeal point of light; Transcendental Meditation reframes pure consciousness as a scientized 'unified field'; Osho's vision is non-dual and anti-ascetic. What recurs is the reinterpretation of classical Hindu concepts (Brahman, kundalini, kalpa, avatar) for a modern, often universalist and 'scientific' audience.
dossier.practice: Each centers on an initiatory technique and guru-devotion: mantra meditation (TM), Raja Yoga with celibacy and vegetarianism (Brahma Kumaris), Kriya Yoga (Self-Realization Fellowship), dynamic meditation and sannyas (Osho), kundalini 'self-realization' (Sahaja Yoga), and deity worship with chanting of the Hare Krishna mantra and temple life (ISKCON). Several have been globally influential and, in several cases, publicly controversial.
dossier.canon: Founder-authored texts rather than a shared scripture — Prabhupada's 'Bhagavad-gita As It Is' (1968), the Brahma Kumaris' channeled 'Murlis,' Maharishi Mahesh Yogi's 'Science of Being and Art of Living' (1963), and Paramahansa Yogananda's 'Autobiography of a Yogi' (1946) — layered over classical sources such as the Bhagavad Gita, Upanishads, and Yoga Sutras.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds the portable technique and the living guru — liberation repackaged as a transmissible, marketable method that crosses cultures, detaching yogic practice from its inherited social and ritual context.
sources: Lola Williamson, 'Transcendent in America: Hindu-Inspired Meditation Movements as New Religion' (2010)
Hugh B. Urban, 'Zorba the Buddha: Sex, Spirituality, and the New Global Capitalism in the Rajneesh Movement' (2015)
Elizabeth De Michelis, 'A History of Modern Yoga: Patanjali and Western Esotericism' (2004)
Kim Knott, 'Hinduism: A Very Short Introduction' (2nd ed., 2016)
